scenarios.question: During Piaget's Preoperational stage, children primarily learn through:
options: Symbolic thinking, egocentrism, and magical reasoning
scenarios.rationale: The preoperational stage (2-7 years) features symbolic thinking, egocentrism (difficulty seeing others' perspectives), and magical/animistic reasoning. Conservation is not yet mastered. (Slide 8)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: be noncompetitive Helps the child adjust to an expanding world and inc
Exclude family members from care decisions
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
Use the same approach as with adult patients
scenarios.rationale: be noncompetitive Helps the child adjust to an expanding world and inc. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 25)

scenarios.rationale: Art therapy Overall, play Should be noncompetitive Helps the child adj. Types of Play Therapeutic Play therapy Art therapy Overall, play Should be nonco... (Slide 25)

scenarios.question: Preschoolers engage in magical thinking and believe their thoughts can cause events. This is:
scenarios.rationale: Piaget's preoperational stage (2-7 years) features magical thinking, egocentrism, animism. Concrete operations come at 7-11 years. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A 4-year-old asks many 'why' questions. This is:
scenarios.rationale: Preschoolers are curious and ask 'why' constantly — this is normal. It reflects their preoperational thinking and need to understand. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Preschoolers often engage in which type of play?
scenarios.rationale: Preschoolers (3-5): associative play (interacting, sharing), cooperative play (organized with rules). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A preschooler who can draw a person with 6 body parts demonstrates:
scenarios.rationale: Drawing a person with 6+ parts: 5 years. 3 parts: 3-4 years. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which fear is common in preschoolers?
options: Fear of the dark, monsters, animals
scenarios.rationale: Preschoolers have vivid imaginations → fears of dark, monsters, animals, separation. Address with reassurance. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A 4-year-old tells elaborate stories that are not true. This is:
scenarios.rationale: Preschoolers blend reality and fantasy. This is normal imaginative development, not lying. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Preschoolers should brush teeth:
scenarios.rationale: Parents supervise/assist brushing until age 6-8. Use pea-sized fluoride toothpaste. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which is an appropriate expectation for a 5-year-old?
scenarios.rationale: Tying shoelaces: 5-6 years. Reading simple words: 5-6 years. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Preschoolers often have imaginary friends. This indicates:
scenarios.rationale: Imaginary friends are normal in 3-5 year olds. They help children practice social skills. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the recommended daily screen time for preschoolers?
scenarios.rationale: AAP: <1 hour/day quality programming for ages 2-5. No screens under 18 months except video calls. (Standard NCLEX)
